baseline_dominance.justification: Construction argues AS is adversarial (§3.1) and that RFC 8693+DPoP cannot provide auditable delegation without exposing intermediate scopes/participants when AS colludes. spiffe_engineer partially concedes graph-topology hiding is beyond WIMSE. However, rfc7662_advocate Attack 1 shows no formal distinguishing game where a forging AS succeeds against RFC 8693+signed JWT yet fails against the ZK circuit. The baseline can approximate for trusted-AS deployments (the majority case per auth0_pm Attack 4). Preferable but not provably beyond baseline.

formal_security_argument.justification: Threat model is explicit (§3.1 AS adversarial), assumptions are named (A5 KZG SRS, Poseidon, PLONK ZK), and Games 1-4 provide reduction sketches. However, cryptographer Attack 1 shows the core 'narrowing' property lacks a formal scope lattice definition — the reduction is over an undefined relation, making the narrowing claim 'an assertion, not a theorem.' Attack 4 shows Game 4 invokes HVZK but needs SE-PLONK (AGM+ROM) for the malicious-verifier journalist scenario. Threat model stated but reduction has critical definitional gaps.

implementability.justification: Construction maps to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon hash, PLONK agent circuit, BabyJubjub EdDSA, ElGamal escrow. Constraints C1-C14 are enumerated. However, auth0_pm Attack 1 shows ~15s prove time per hop yields 75s for a 5-hop AI pipeline — structurally excluding the primary named scenario. No batching, recursive folding (Nova/SuperNova), or deferred-audit model is proposed. Maps to primitives but cost is prohibitive for stated use case.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 found multiple attacks the construction cannot answer: (1) cryptographer Attack 1 — scope lattice undefined, adversary can satisfy C2 without semantic narrowing; (2) cryptographer Attack 2 — adversarial AS substitutes pk_reg in C14, no key authenticity mechanism; (3) cryptographer Attack 3 — cross-session scope correlation via equal commitments, no unlinkability game; (4) spiffe_engineer Attack 3 — phantom workloads satisfy all constraints without corresponding real workloads; (5) auth0_pm Attack 3 — RegulatorReveal inverts compliance requirements (NCUA needs identity disclosure, not ZK proof of structural integrity). These are unanswerable without construction changes.

scenario_fit.justification: Two scenarios named: AI pipeline audit and journalist/source chain. NCUA examiner is a named stakeholder in Scenario A. However, auth0_pm Attack 1 shows the AI pipeline is structurally excluded by latency. auth0_pm Attack 3 shows NCUA examiners need identity disclosure under 12 CFR Part 748, making the privacy guarantee a liability. cu_ciso Attacks 1-4 show Scenario A lacks escrow key custody, examiner-facing artifact format, vendor-onboardable trust setup, and operational runbook. Scenarios named but deployment viability is undermined across both.

gaps: Define a formal scope lattice (L, ≤) as a circuit public parameter with a narrowing predicate C_narrow enforcing scope[i] ≤_L scope[i-1], and state the soundness game over this predicate
Add SE-PLONK security assumption (AGM+ROM) to support Game 4 indistinguishability against malicious verifiers in Scenario B
Bind pk_reg to a transparency log or require PoK(sk_reg) committed to the constraint system to prevent adversarial-AS key substitution in C14
Add a session-unlinkability game with per-proof random blinding to prevent cross-session scope correlation (cryptographer Attack 3)
Require hop commitments to include a verifiable workload identity binding, or prove phantom hops cannot expand scope, to close the phantom-workload attack

Provide a concrete latency solution: batch/deferred audit model, recursive SNARK folding (Nova), or explicit statement that proofs are generated offline — otherwise the AI pipeline scenario is structurally excluded
Add a formal distinguishing game where a forging AS succeeds against RFC 8693+signed JWT introspection but fails against the ZK circuit, to justify baseline dominance

Resolve the compliance inversion: either restrict Scenario A to internal audit (not NCUA) or add a human-readable, legally admissible disclosure layer on top of C14 that satisfies 12 CFR Part 748 identity disclosure requirements
Specify escrow key custody, rotation, and audit logging for C14 to satisfy NCUA Part 748 Appendix B and GLBA Safeguards Rule
Address the KZG trusted setup procurement problem: make IPA-PLONK/STARK the primary backend for Scenario A, or provide vendor-onboarding artifacts for the SRS dependency
